title: Dinner rolls
subtitle: Soft pull-apart white rolls for the table
excerpt: Twenty soft white rolls baked close together in a tin so they rise into each other and pull apart at the table. An enriched dough with butter and a little egg gives a tender crumb and a thin, pliable crust.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: the plain white roll method. Hannah Glasse, The Art of Cookery (1747), Project Gutenberg: enriched milk-roll precedent. The pull-apart tin arrangement became common in British household baking by the late 19th century.

content.text: Pull-apart rolls sit in a close-packed tin for the final proof, pressing against each other as they rise. The result is a soft side crust where each roll touches its neighbour, and a proper crust only on the exposed top and ends. This is the
version: butter and egg in the mix keep the crumb tender.

attrs.prepNote: beaten with a pinch of salt, for glazing

content.text: Combine the flour, yeast, salt, and sugar in a large bowl, keeping the yeast and salt on opposite sides of the bowl at first. Add the butter and rub it into the flour until the mixture resembles rough breadcrumbs. Add the beaten egg, then pour in the warm milk and water. Mix to a shaggy dough, then turn out onto an unfloured surface and knead for 10 minutes until smooth and elastic. The dough will be slightly tacky; resist adding flour, which would tighten the crumb. In a stand mixer with a dough hook, 6 minutes on medium speed.

for an enriched dough runs slower than a lean dough. Place the dough in a lightly oiled bowl, cover with a damp cloth, and leave at room temperature until doubled in size. This takes around 60 minutes in a warm kitchen; allow up to 90 minutes in a cool room.

Line a 30×20 cm baking tin with baking paper. Knock back the dough gently and divide into 20 even pieces of about 42 g each. Shape each piece into a tight ball by cupping the dough under your palm and dragging it in small circles against the work surface. Arrange in the tin in four rows of five, leaving a 5 mm gap between each roll. Cover loosely with oiled cling film and leave to prove for 30 minutes; the rolls should be touching and puffed.

Preheat the oven to 200°C conventional. Brush the tops gently with beaten egg, taking care not to deflate the rolls. Bake for 18 to 20 minutes until deep golden on top and the rolls pull away cleanly from the tin sides. The internal temperature should read 93 to 96°C on a probe. Leave to cool in the tin for 5 minutes before tipping out.
